Nvidia's AI takeover starts now. Openclaw and Nemlaw. So Jason Huang just stood on a stage in San Jose and said something that should stop you in your tracks. He said every company in the world needs an openclaw strategy. Not a suggestion, not a considerate. He said it the same way people used to say you need a Linux strategy or a HTTP strategy and everyone who didn't have those strategies got left behind. So let's talk about what just happened. Nvidia, Nema claw, openclaw and why this matter, why this moment matters more than most people realize. Here's a quick version of what openlaw actually is. Because the real story is what Nvidia just did to it. You're not copying and pasting answers for open core. It acts. It logs in services, fills out forms, monitors systems, and reports back through the same chat thread that you use to talk to your friends. Now, most AI tools are like a smart friend. You text for advice. You ask, they answer, you go do it yourself. Open Claw does the actual thing. So, if you tell it, check my emails or for example, build this website, it will actually go off do it without you touching a button. Right? And that's happening already now with the growth of open claw. I mean for example Peter Steinber actually published it over a weekend. Late January 2026 he crosses over 100,000 GitHub stars in under two weeks. By March the 3rd 2026 it's over 250,000 stars surpassing React B and every other software project on GitHub. React took 10 years. OpenClaw did it in 60 days and Jensen TWang called it openclaw opened the next frontier of AI to everyone and became the fastest growing open source project in history. And then he said this Mac and Windows are the operating systems for the personal computer. OpenClaw is the operating system for personal AI. This is the moment the industry has been waiting for the beginning of a new renaissance in software. He's comparing OpenClaw to the moment personal computers got operating systems. Before Windows and Mac OS, computers existed, but most people couldn't use it. The OS made it accessible to everyone. Jensen is saying OpenClaw does the same thing for AI agents. And now Nvidia just made it work for serious large scale use. That's what Neocore is. So Nvidia developed Nemoclaw, an enterprisegrade AI agent platform built on top of OpenClaw. It's essentially OpenClaw with enterprisegrade features baked in. So it turns openclaw into a platform enterprises can tap into with one command. Open claw was already powerful but rough. Setting up was complex. Running it safely at scale was complicated. For big companies that was a dealbreaker. Neoclaw solves that. Is open claw polished for the real world. Nvidia's Carrie Briski VP of generative AI software for enterprise put it clearly. Claws are autonomous agents that can plan act and implement tasks on their own. And they've gone from just thinking and implementing on tasks to achieving entire missions. We use to prompt with one how or why. But for clause now we prompt with build, create or make. Read that again. We used to tell AI what to do. Now we tell it to build something and it figures out everything in between on its own. It's a massive shift. How does Nemclaw actually work? Well, you want to think of it like a ward garden. Neoclor installs with a single command using Nvidia's agent toolkit. It also installs open shell which provides an isolated sandbox adding data privacy controls to autonomous agents. This is the missing infrastructure layer beneath clause giving them the access they need to be productive whilst enforcing policy based security network and privacy guardrails. Openclaw is a brilliant fast employee who never sleeps. But if you hand them your company keys and say do whatever you need, well that could be chaos, right? Neoclaw defines the guardrails. The agent could do its job without touching things it shouldn't. And Neml uses any coding agent and can tap open models including Nvidia Neatron running locally on the user system through a privacy router. Agents can also access frontier cloud models. So local for private data, cloud for the heavy lifting and you get the best of both worlds. And it runs on hardware that most people already own. So for example, GeForce, RTX PCs, workstations, and Optiful data center machines. So Nvidia isn't saying like, you know, get the most expensive kit here. They're saying use what you've got. And that's a move to get this into as many hands as possible as fast as possible. One more piece of the story that's worth knowing here. Nvidia worked directly with OpenClaw's creator Peter Steinber to develop Neoclaw. This isn't like some sort of deal together that that's the creator of the fastest growing open-source project in history. Now at OpenAI collaborating with Jensen Hang to build the enterprise version. Three of the biggest forces in AI all converging on the same platform. Now zoom out because Jensen didn't just announce the product. He made a decoration right said for the CEOs the question is what's your open core strategy we need it we all have a lineup strategy we all needed to have a http html strategy which started the internet think about the companies that had no internet strategy in 1998 a lot of them don't exist anymore he also said that after openclaw it will shift from SAS into AI acting as a service instead of subscribing to like 20 different tools you have AI agents that do what those tools used to do one agent handling your CRM, your emails, your reporting, your scheduling, no app switching, no copy pasting. The agent handles it itself. And Nvidia says you can expect 10 million digital workers alongside humans in the future. 10 million AI agents, not replacing people wholesale, working alongside them, handling the repetitive, the routine, the time consuming, freeing humans for the work that actually requires a person. The people who learn to direct agents well are going to be extraordinarily productive. The people who don't will be doing manually what everyone else is automating. Yes, Nemlaw is currently in alpha. Nvidia stated, "Expect rough edges. We're building toward production ready sandbox orchestration, but the starting point is getting your own environment up and running." Linux launched rough. The iPhone launched without an app store. The rough launch is the beginning, not the reason to dismiss it. So, what do you have to do with this? If you run a business, start answering the question Jansen answered, right, which is what is your open core strategy? Not should you have one? What is it? Look at what your team does repeatedly every day, every week. The predictable pattern based tasks. That's where agents start. If you're a creator, a marketer, an operator, experiment with Open now. It's free. It's open source. The people learning now will be the ones who know how to use these tools when the polished version ships. Because here's the bottom line. AI is 60% of Nvidia's business now. The most powerful hardware company on the planet is going all in on AI agent infrastructure. Jensen Wang is standing on stage comparing this to the internet. The age of AI as a chalk chatbot that you talk to is ending. The age of AI agents that work for you around the clock across your tools and data is beginning. OpenClaw was a proof of concept built by one person in an afternoon became the fastest growing open source project in history. Prove the world is hungry for AI that actually does things. Nemo claw is the infrastructure response and video saying we see where this is going and we're building the roads and Peter Syberger said it simply openclaw brings people closer to AI and helps create a world where everyone has their own AI agents everyone. Not just big companies not just engineers everyone. The tools are here the infrastructure is being built and video is all in. The only question left is where are you in this?
